original_abstract: Enantioselective allylation is a cornerstone of organic synthesis, frequently employed to generate chiral homoallylic alcohols and amines. These compounds are essential building blocks for constructing a diverse array of natural products and therapeutic agents. Over the past decade, the field has seen a surge in research focused specifically on the catalytic asymmetric allylation of carbonyl compounds and imines using allylic boronates. This review explores recent advancements in the area, highlighting the evolution from stoichiometric reagents to highly efficient catalytic systems, and discusses the development of metal-mediated, acid-catalyzed, and organocatalytic methods.
humanized_abstract: Enantioselective allylation is a frequently used method in organic synthesis for producing chiral homoallylic alcohols and amines. These molecules are components of a large number of biologically significant natural products and drugs. The research in the enantioselective catalytic allylation of carbonyl compounds and imines using allylic boronates over the last decade is summarized. The progress from the use of simple catalysts for the use of highly efficient catalytic systems is discussed, as well as the transition from metal-based to acid-based, and organocatalytic approaches.

The research in the enantioselective catalytic allylation of carbonyl compounds and imines using allylic boronates over the last decade is summarized. The progress from the use of simple catalysts for the use of highly efficient catalytic systems is discussed, as well as the transition from metal-based to acid-based, and organocatalytic approaches.
